method: Compiled from primary sources (CMS, KFF, Congress.gov, CRS, Public Citizen, PhRMA, Sidley/Jones Day legal analyses) and cross-checked against the underlying documents.

facts.statement: Under the IRA's Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program, small-molecule drugs (FDA New Drug Application) become eligible for selection 7 years after approval, with the negotiated maximum fair price (MFP) taking effect at year 9; biologics (Biologics License Application) are eligible at year 11 with the MFP effective at year 13.

The four-year difference between when negotiated prices take effect for small molecules (year 9) and biologics (year 13) is commonly called the 'pill penalty'; critics say it creates a structural incentive to develop biologics over small-molecule pills.

The Ensuring Pathways to Innovative Cures (EPIC) Act, H.R. 1492 (119th Congress), would require small-molecule drugs to have at least 11 years of market approval (matching the biologic timeline) before becoming eligible for selection, eliminating the 4-year gap.
H.R. 1492 (EPIC Act) was introduced February 25, 2025 with a Senate companion (S. 832); as of 2026 it remains pending and has not been enacted.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025, broadened the IRA's orphan-drug exclusion from a single rare-disease indication to drugs whose only approved indication(s) are for one or more rare diseases, so 'serial orphan' drugs with multiple rare-disease indications and no non-orphan approval are exempt from negotiation.

For orphan drugs that later gain a non-orphan approval, the OBBBA resets the negotiation-eligibility clock to begin the day after the first non-orphan approval (not the original FDA approval), substantially delaying negotiation; effective for initial price applicability year 2028 and after.
facts.numeric_value: Clock resets to first non-orphan approval; IPAY 2028+

facts.statement: The Congressional Budget Office estimated the OBBBA's expanded orphan-drug exemption would cost Medicare about $8.8 billion over ten years.

Public Citizen argues the 'pill penalty' is 'nothing more than a pharma industry myth,' citing research that venture-capital investment in small molecules has not dropped since the negotiation program passed.

Public Citizen states that had the EPIC Act's delay been in effect, Medicare would have been barred from negotiating 5 of the first 10 drugs (>$37B in annual gross Part D spending) and 8 of the second 15 drugs (>$28B).
Public Citizen further states that 6 of the 8 (75%) small-molecule drugs selected for 2026 — more than $11B in annual Medicare spending — would have been excluded under an EPIC-style delay.

Industry (PhRMA/PHAR, 2023): 61% of the 31 cancer drugs approved 2006-2012 gained at least one new indication after launch, and ~40% of those arrived 7+ years out — inside the window the year-9 clock truncates.
facts.numeric_value: 61% of 31 cancer drugs; ~40% of new indications at 7+ yrs

facts.statement: A University of Chicago analysis projects roughly 188 fewer small-molecule medicines as a result of the 9-vs-13 gap.
facts.source_org: University of Chicago analysis (cited via Epstein Becker Green)

facts.statement: An independent, peer-reviewed study (Patterson et al., National Pharmaceutical Council, Therapeutic Innovation & Regulatory Science, 2024) found 25% of oncology drugs received their most recent subsequent indication only after they would be negotiation-eligible.
facts.numeric_value: 25% of oncology drugs (subsequent indication after eligibility)

facts.statement: KFF finds that drugs which would be ineligible for negotiation under a delayed (biologic-style) timeline accounted for about two-thirds of total gross Medicare Part D spending on the first 25 selected drugs — $61 billion of $91 billion.
facts.numeric_value: ~2/3 of Part D spend on 25 drugs ($61B of $91B)
